text: These complex surgeries require multiple stages and, again, are often a last resort.
We then resuscitated the patient, performed an in-cervical diversion, and later on reconstructed her with a long segment supercharged pedicle jejunal interposition.
Positioning in pre-op is very important when preparing to perform long segment esophageal replacement.
I advocate prepping the groins into the place for potential saphenous vein harvest in the event that adequate length of the vessels is not provided.
I also recommend prepping the feeding tube into the field such that it can be removed and later replaced.
Prepping the neck, chest, abdomen, and groins is an adequate prep for such procedure.
The incision begins with a chevron.
I do not advocate that a chevron incision be performed.
However, in this particular case, we used it because it was the former incision.
A left oblique neck incision extending down onto the chest is performed.
The jejunal feeding tube is taken down with sharp dissection using scissors, and the jejunal defect is closed.
Bowel translumination is a very important part of this procedure.
By placing a light behind the bowel, one can delineate the mesenteric arcade.
Bowel transillumination is seen in this image.
We are labeling the specific arcades such that the first arcade will be left
with its native vessels supplying the ligament of trites.
The second arcade will be anastomose to the left internal mammary artery and vein.
The third arcade will be ligated right after it takes off and will be used as a bridging arcade.
In between the third and fourth, or in between the second and third, the mesentery can be divided all the way up to the bowel wall.
Once the pedicle is prepared, all of the other portions of the surgery must be completed prior to transposition.
The neck dissection includes the dissection to remove the head of the clavicle and a quarter of the manubrium.
Sharp and blunt dissection is performed in this area.

One must take care when dividing the cartilaginous structures to know that the internal mammary artery and vein are directly below that junction of the bony segment of the rib and the cartilaginous portion.
Once the capsule is removed from the clavicular sternal junction, one can then begin to remove that segment of the clavicle with a rongeur.
The rongeur is an effective way to remove the bony segments.
Today, we also like to use a Midas Rex, which will soften the edges of the bone.
If the edges of the bone are not softened, they can wear into the vessels and cause bleeding.
The end esophagostomy is also taken down using an elliptical incision.
The longitudinal incision going in a vertical fashion through the anterior left chest can also be directed to incorporate an elliptical incision and excision of the end ostomy.
A sternal saw is used to divide the manubrium and resect that portion of the manubrium, the head of the left side of the first rib, and the head of the clavicle.
Two teams can work both at the neck and at the abdomen to facilitate an efficient surgery.
the plastic surgery team will often assist or primarily harvest the mammary vessels and prepare them for a microvascular anastomosis.
Using small plastic Ackland clamps, the team can clamp the artery in the vein and divide them.
Once that is performed, a created tunnel using an incision on the base of the diaphragm
going in the center of the upper portion of the abdomen and then tunneling anterior to the heart can lead you up to the area that has been opened in the left anterior chest.
Jejunal testing and ligation can also be performed using the Doppler.
A finger palpation can allow the surgeon to make sure that there's adequate collateral blood flow prior to the final division of the artery for a mammary anastomosis.
By inspecting the arcades and making sure that the collateral flow is adequate prior to dividing the last artery, the surgeon measures twice, cuts once.
Often as the third arcade is divided, clips are used or a stapling device.
A hand tied suture is also recommended for those portions of the bowel artery that will stay in the abdomen.
Delivering the jejunum to the neck is performed by taking a Penrose drain attached to a bag.
The bag that is attached to the Penrose drain will protect the bowel as it is brought up into the neck.
In a pushing manner rather than a pulling manner, the bowel bag protecting the bowel with the aclin clamps on the mesenteric arteries will be delivered through the neck.
One must take care not to damage the left internal mammary artery and vein while this is being delivered.
They are directly below the piece of bowel.
Looking up into the chest, one can also see that the diaphragm is not impinging on the bowel.
The ischemia clock starts once the mesenteric artery has been divided.
The microscopic anastomosis is then performed.
A 3.5 mm size venous coupler is often used when creating the microvascular venous anastomosis.
The coupler has two small plastic edges with spears through which the vein is everted on top of.
After the vein is everted and landed on top of the spears, the coupling device is closed
and the two vessels, the vein to the mesentery and the left internal mammary vein, are connected.
During this portion of the surgery, the spears are holding the left internal mammary vein on the right and the mesenteric vein on the left.
Under the microscope, the two structures are joined as the coupler is closed.
Once the coupler is completely closed, the veins are connected.
The clamps can be removed and the blood flow is established.
A microvascular arterial anastomosis is most typically performed using 9-0 nylon suture.
An interrupted individual sutured anastomosis is created with the microvascular augmentation.
The microscope is used while suturing with the 9-0 nylon suture in an interrupted fashion.
The clamps are removed and Doppler examination of the adequacy of the anastomosis is performed.
One must make sure that the way that the vessels lay
does not cause kinking, and the additional portion of the bowel is resected.
The bowel anastomosis can be performed either at the beginning or at the end, before the microvascular anastomosis or after.
There are two options when creating a monitoring flap.
When closing the neck, drains are placed.
One must take care not to place the drains directly on top of the anastomosis, as in some cases we believe this led to an increased leak rate.

Now that we are placing our drains far below the level of the anastomosis, but in an adequate position to drain a leak if it were to happen, we have noticed that we have hardly ever had any more leaks.
The drains are sutured into place.
The skin incision is closed.
The remaining portion of the mesenteric fat can be used to cover and position the microvascular anastomosis.
The end ostomy is also closed.
In some cases, there may be too much bulge to close all of the skin and a small portion of this can be left open.
When closing the diaphragm defect, it will be important to make sure that there is no area left for paraconduit herniation.

While one would not want to take a suture to damage the mesenteric artery as a pedicle going up to the midjejunum in the chest, at the same time, a very snug diaphragm defect closure should be performed, taking small bites of the mesentery to close against the edge of the diaphragm such that no space is left for potential herniation of bowel.
This illustration shows the bowel transposed up to the level of the neck with a picture of the arterial and venous anastomosis.
The venous coupling device can be seen in the right lower hand portion of the screen.
Monitoring flap can be seen on the right, and to the left, the mesenteric arterial preparation.
It is important to note that this is not a one surgeon case.
A multidisciplinary team with a very fine-tuned cooperative dance is required
in order to obtain optimal outcomes.
Years of training and fellowships and superfellowships are required in order to adequately train to perform complex long-segment jejunal interposition.
Mentorship, learning from others' mistakes, and standing on the shoulders of giants will allow teams to work together effectively to recreate the future of esophageal reconstruction.
